title: Reinforcement Learning & Bandits in Practice
pitchHtml: Reinforcement learning (RL) is the part of AI that learns by trial and error: an agent takes actions, gets rewards, and adjusts to earn more reward over time. It is genuinely powerful — it is how game-playing systems beat humans, how robots learn control, and how LLMs are tuned to be helpful (RLHF). It is also, for the overwhelming majority of business problems, the wrong tool — expensive to set up, hard to evaluate, dangerous to debug, and almost always beaten by either supervised learning or its lighter cousin, the bandit. This module gives you the vocabulary to recognise the rare genuine fit, the judgment to de-escalate everything else, and a working command of bandits — the "RL lite" that actually earns its keep in recommendation, content, and pricing. The throughline, stated once and repeated on purpose: RL is powerful but usually overkill — reach for a bandit or supervised learning unless the problem truly needs sequential decisions.

items.html: Describe the RL setup (agent, environment, state, action, reward, policy, return) and the trial-and-error loop in plain terms
Read the canonical RL formulas — return, the Bellman idea, the Q-learning update — and explain what each one says without deriving it
Decide when a problem is a bandit (one-shot choice), full RL (sequential decisions), or just supervised learning in disguise
Explain why a contextual bandit beats a fixed A/B test for recommendation, content, and offer selection
State the high bar full RL must clear — simulator or massive data, a definable reward, tolerance for exploration cost
Name the headline RL risks (reward hacking, safety, evaluation difficulty, non-stationarity) and why most "RL" problems should not be RL
Scope a bandit-first personalisation or pricing mission and say plainly what you will not promise

subsections.title: The RL Setup — Learning by Trial and Error

blocks.html: RL is decision-making under feedback. The cast is small and worth memorising, because every RL conversation uses these words:

TermPlain meaningExample (a pricing agent)
AgentThe learner / decision-makerThe pricing system
EnvironmentThe world it acts in and gets feedback fromThe market + customers
State sssWhat the agent observes right nowDemand, inventory, competitor price
Action aaaA choice it can makeSet price to €X
Reward rrrA scalar feedback signal after actingMargin earned on the sale
Policy π\piπThe agent's strategy: state → action"If inventory high, discount"
Return GGGTotal reward accumulated over timeCumulative margin over the season

The loop is the whole idea: the agent observes a state, picks an action by its policy, the environment returns a reward and a new state, and the agent updates its policy to do better next time. Repeat thousands to millions of times.

blocks.text:
┌───────────── reward r, next state s' ──────────────┐
        ▼                                                    │
   [ AGENT ] ── action a ──▶ [ ENVIRONMENT ] ────────────────┘
   (policy π)                 (the world)
        ▲                                                     
        └────────────── observes state s ─────────────────────

blocks.html:
Two structural distinctions a leader should know:

  • Episodic vs continuing. Episodic tasks have a natural end — a game ends, a customer session finishes, a delivery completes; learning happens over many episodes. Continuing tasks run forever (a control loop, a live recommender) with no terminal state. The distinction changes how you measure success and, as we'll see, why discounting matters.
  • Reward is the spec. In supervised learning you give the model the right answers (labels). In RL you give it a reward signal and let it figure out which actions earn it. That single difference is the source of most of RL's power and most of its danger — define the reward badly and the agent will exploit your definition, not your intent (see §9).

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: RL is what you reach for when there are no labels, only consequences — when the "right answer" isn't known up front but you can score outcomes after the fact, and where today's action changes tomorrow's situation. If neither of those holds, you do not have an RL problem, and most "RL" pitches don't.
The agent's goal is to maximise expected cumulative reward — not the reward of the next action, but the total over time. That total, seen from time ttt, is the return:
Gt=∑k≥0γk rt+k=rt+γrt+1+γ2rt+2+⋯G_t = \sum_{k \ge 0} \gamma^k\, r_{t+k} = r_t + \gamma r_{t+1} + \gamma^2 r_{t+2} + \cdotsGt​=k≥0∑​γkrt+k​=rt​+γrt+1​+γ2rt+2​+⋯

where rt+kr_{t+k}rt+k​ is the reward kkk steps into the future and γ∈[0,1)\gamma \in [0,1)γ∈[0,1) is the discount factor — a knob that down-weights rewards the further off they are. With γ=0.9\gamma = 0.9γ=0.9, a reward two steps away counts for 0.810.810.81 of its face value; ten steps away, about 0.350.350.35.

Why discount at all?

  • Far-future reward is uncertain — the world drifts, the model is imperfect; a reward now is worth more than a maybe-reward later (the same logic as a financial discount rate).
  • It keeps the maths finite. For a continuing task that never ends, the undiscounted sum of rewards can be infinite and meaningless; γ<1\gamma < 1γ<1 makes the return a finite, comparable number.
  • It encodes how far-sighted you want the agent to be. Low γ\gammaγ → greedy, short-horizon behaviour; high γ\gammaγ → patient, long-horizon behaviour. It is a business choice as much as a technical one.

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: γ\gammaγ is the agent's patience dial. Think of it as "how many future steps do I really care about?" — roughly 1/(1−γ)1/(1-\gamma)1/(1−γ) steps of effective horizon (γ=0.9⇒≈10\gamma=0.9 \Rightarrow \approx 10γ=0.9⇒≈10 steps; γ=0.99⇒≈100\gamma=0.99 \Rightarrow \approx 100γ=0.99⇒≈100). Set it by the decision's real time-horizon, not by default.

To act well, the agent estimates how good a situation is. Two related quantities do this:

  • State value Vπ(s)V^\pi(s)Vπ(s) — the expected return if you start in state sss and follow policy π\piπ thereafter. "How good is it to be here?"
  • Action value Qπ(s,a)Q^\pi(s,a)Qπ(s,a) — the expected return if you take action aaa in state sss, then follow π\piπ. "How good is it to do this here?" QQQ is the more useful of the two, because comparing QQQ across actions tells you which action to pick.

The Bellman idea is the recursive insight that makes RL tractable: the value of a state equals the immediate reward plus the discounted value of wherever you land next.
Vπ(s)=E[ r+γ Vπ(s′) ]V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}\big[\, r + \gamma\, V^\pi(s') \,\big]Vπ(s)=E[r+γVπ(s′)]

where rrr is the reward you get and s′s's′ is the next state. In words: value now = reward now + discounted value of next. That is the whole trick — a long-horizon problem is broken into "one step + the rest," so the agent never has to reason over the entire future at once.

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: Bellman is dynamic programming wearing an RL hat: "the best way from here" is "the best next step, plus the best way from wherever that lands me." It is the same self-referential structure as a chess engine valuing a position by the positions it leads to. We state it as an intuition, not a theorem — the convergence proofs and the full optimality theory live in the AI Scientist Path (Reinforcement Learning Foundations).
The single most recognisable RL algorithm is Q-learning. It learns Q(s,a)Q(s,a)Q(s,a) from experience, one step at a time, with this update — worth being able to read on a whiteboard:

Q(s,a)  ←  Q(s,a)+α [ r+γmax⁡a′Q(s′,a′)−Q(s,a)⏟TD-error ]Q(s,a) \;\leftarrow\; Q(s,a) + \alpha\,\big[\,\underbrace{r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)}_{\text{TD-error}}\,\big]Q(s,a)←Q(s,a)+α[TD-errorr+γa′max​Q(s′,a′)−Q(s,a)​​]

Term by term:

  • Q(s,a)Q(s,a)Q(s,a) — current estimate of the value of doing aaa in sss.
  • α∈(0,1]\alpha \in (0,1]α∈(0,1] — the learning rate: how big a step to take toward the new evidence. High α\alphaα learns fast but jitters; low α\alphaα is stable but slow.
  • r+γmax⁡a′Q(s′,a′)r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a')r+γmaxa′​Q(s′,a′) — the target: the reward you just got, plus the discounted value of the best action available in the next state.
  • The bracketed quantity is the TD-error (temporal-difference error): the gap between what you expected (Q(s,a)Q(s,a)Q(s,a)) and what you now estimate you'll get (the target). It is the surprise.

So the rule reads: nudge the old estimate toward the target, by a fraction α\alphaα of the surprise. Do this across millions of state-action experiences and QQQ converges toward telling you the best action in every state.

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: This is "learn from the gap between expectation and reality," the same shape as every error-correcting update on this path (the gradient-descent step in Deep Learning Essentials is its cousin). The max⁡a′\max_{a'}maxa′​ is what makes it learn the optimal policy even while exploring sub-optimally — it always bootstraps off the best next move, not the one actually taken. You do not implement this as a tech lead; you recognise it, and you recognise that it needs vast amounts of trial-and-error experience to fill in QQQ — which is exactly why it needs a simulator or huge interaction data (§8).

subsections.title: Exploration vs Exploitation — the Central Tension

blocks.html:
An agent that always picks the action it currently thinks is best (exploitation) will never discover that a different action is actually better. An agent that always tries random things (exploration) never cashes in what it has learned. Good RL balances the two. The simplest, most common scheme is ε-greedy:

With probability ε\varepsilonε, pick a random action (explore); otherwise pick the best-known action (exploit). Often ε\varepsilonε starts high and decays as the agent learns.

This tension is not a footnote — it is the core difficulty of every learning-by-acting system, and it is the reason RL costs real money in production: every exploratory action is, by definition, a knowingly sub-optimal choice you are paying for in the hope of learning something. In a game simulator that cost is free. On live customers, it is a discount you gave away, a worse recommendation you served, an offer you shouldn't have made. Budget for exploration cost explicitly — it is the line item clients forget.

You will hear these names. You need to recognise them and know roughly what each trades off — not implement them. Depth and derivations: AI Scientist Path Reinforcement Learning Foundations and Deep Reinforcement Learning.

FamilyIdea (intuition only)Named examplesWhen it fits
Value-basedLearn Q(s,a)Q(s,a)Q(s,a), act greedily on itQ-learning; DQN (deep Q-network: a neural net approximates QQQ over large/continuous states)Discrete actions; the workhorse for "which action is best here"
Policy-basedLearn the policy π\piπ directly, no value tablePolicy gradient; PPO (proximal policy optimisation — the modern default, and the algorithm behind RLHF)Continuous or high-dimensional actions; stable training
Actor-criticCombine both: an actor picks actions, a critic scores themA2C, PPO (also actor-critic), SACMost modern deep RL; best of both worlds
Model-free vs model-basedModel-free learns purely from experience; model-based also learns a model of the environment to plan against(axis, not an algorithm)Model-based is more sample-efficient but harder; most applied RL is model-free

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: "Deep RL" just means a neural network is doing the value or policy approximation, so the agent can handle states too large to tabulate (raw pixels, long histories). That power is exactly what makes it data-hungry, unstable to train, and hard to debug — the gains and the pains come from the same source. For a leader, the only name you must connect to your own world is PPO, because it is the engine of RLHF (tuning LLMs to human preferences — see Large Language Models in Practice).

subsections.title: Multi-Armed & Contextual Bandits — the Practical Sweet Spot

blocks.html:
This is the section that pays the rent. A bandit is RL stripped down to its most useful, tractable special case: a single decision, scored immediately, with no sequential state to worry about. No discounting, no Bellman recursion, no exploding state space — just "choose among options, learn which pays best, keep choosing better." For the recommendation, content, ad, offer, and pricing problems most businesses actually have, the bandit is the right tool and full RL is overkill.

  • Multi-armed bandit (MAB). The name is a metaphor: a row of slot machines ("one-armed bandits"), each with an unknown payout rate. You pull arms over time and want to maximise total winnings, which means quickly concentrating on the best arm while not under-sampling the others. Each "arm" is an option: a headline variant, a recommended item, a discount level.
  • Contextual bandit. The realistic upgrade: before choosing, you see a context (who the user is, device, time, history) and you learn a policy from context to best arm. "For this user, which of these options is best right now?" This is the form that powers real personalisation.

The bandit's job is the same exploration-vs-exploitation balance from §5, but for a one-shot choice. Two algorithms dominate; recognise both:

  • UCB (Upper Confidence Bound) — pick the arm with the highest optimistic estimate: its current mean plus an exploration bonus that is large when you've tried it little and shrinks as you gather data. "Optimism in the face of uncertainty." The canonical form:
at=arg⁡max⁡a  [ μ^a+cln⁡tna ]a_t = \arg\max_{a}\;\Big[\, \hat{\mu}_a + c\sqrt{\tfrac{\ln t}{n_a}} \,\Big]at​=argamax​[μ^​a​+cna​lnt​​]

where μ^a\hat{\mu}_aμ^​a​ is arm aaa's estimated mean reward, nan_ana​ is how many times it's been tried, ttt is the total pulls so far, and ccc tunes how aggressively you explore. The square-root term is the bonus: big for rarely-tried arms, vanishing for well-tried ones.

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: UCB explores on purpose but not at random — it only gambles on arms it is genuinely uncertain about (few pulls → big bonus), and stops gambling once it knows them. That targeted curiosity is why it beats both pure greedy (never explores) and ε-greedy (explores blindly, including arms it already knows are bad).

• Thompson sampling — keep a probability distribution over each arm's true reward rate; to choose, draw one sample from each arm's distribution and play the arm with the highest sample. Arms you're unsure about have wide distributions, so they sometimes sample high and get tried; arms you've pinned down sample tightly. It is Bayesian, simple to implement, and empirically excellent. Intuition: let each arm bid with a number drawn from its own plausible range, and the more uncertain an arm is, the more often it gets a turn.

The metric for all of this is regret — how much reward you lost versus an oracle that played the best arm every time:
Regret(T)=∑t=1T(μ∗−μat)\text{Regret}(T) = \sum_{t=1}^{T}\big(\mu^{*} - \mu_{a_t}\big)Regret(T)=t=1∑T​(μ∗−μat​​)

where μ∗\mu^{*}μ∗ is the best arm's mean reward and μat\mu_{a_t}μat​​ is the reward of the arm you actually played at step ttt. Lower is better; good bandit algorithms drive average regret toward zero over time (you make fewer mistakes the more you learn). You don't need the bound — just the concept: regret is the price of learning, and the algorithm's job is to make that price small.

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: A bandit is a thermostat for decisions: it continuously shifts traffic toward whatever is winning, instead of waiting for a verdict. That is the entire reason it beats a fixed test.

Why a bandit beats a fixed A/B test. A classic A/B test (see Model Evaluation, Validation & Experimentation) splits traffic fixed (say 50/50) for the whole test, then picks a winner at the end. While it runs, you knowingly send half your traffic to the losing variant — that's the cost of certainty. A bandit instead adaptively reallocates: as evidence accrues, it sends more traffic to the better arm and less to the worse one, minimising regret during the experiment.

Fixed A/B testBandit
Traffic allocationFixed split for the whole runAdaptive — shifts toward the winner as evidence grows
Cost during the testHigh — half of traffic to the loser the whole timeLow — losing arms starved quickly
Best forA clean, one-off causal answer with a clear stopping point and statistical rigourOngoing optimisation where you want to earn while you learn
Watch out forWastes traffic; binary verdictHarder to get a clean causal read; needs care with non-stationarity & many arms

The honest nuance: A/B tests are still better when you need a rigorous, defensible causal conclusion ("did this change cause the lift?") — bandits optimise outcomes, they don't hand you a clean p-value. Use a bandit to maximise reward continuously; use an A/B test to prove a single hypothesis. (Both live in Model Evaluation, Validation & Experimentation.)

blocks.title: homepage hero recommendation
blocks.html:
A retailer has 6 candidate hero products for its homepage and wants the one that maximises click-through, per user.

  1. Naïve A/B: split traffic into 6 equal buckets, run two weeks, declare a winner. You spent two weeks sending 5/6 of traffic to non-winners, and the "winner" is now stale because tastes shifted.
  2. Multi-armed bandit: treat each product as an arm, optimise click-through with Thompson sampling. Within days it concentrates traffic on the top performers, keeps a trickle exploring the rest, and adapts as performance drifts — far less lost click-through than the fixed split.
  3. Contextual bandit: add context (new vs returning, device, season). Now it learns "product C for returning mobile users, product A for new desktop visitors" — personalised, still optimising in real time. This is the production-grade answer, and it is not full RL: each impression is a one-shot decision with immediate feedback and no sequential state.

The leader's takeaway: the moment someone says "personalise it," "optimise the recommendation," "test which offer/headline/price wins," your first thought should be bandit, not RL — and often not even a fixed A/B test.
The bar is high, and you should hold it high. All four of these need to be true before full (sequential) RL is the right call:

ConditionWhy it's requiredRed-flag absence
Decisions are genuinely sequentialToday's action changes tomorrow's state and the reward depends on the sequence, not each choice in isolationIf each decision is independent → it's a bandit, not RL
A simulator OR massive interaction dataRL learns by millions of trials; it cannot learn from a small static datasetNo cheap way to generate experience → RL is infeasible
A reward you can actually define and measureThe agent optimises the reward literally; a fuzzy or proxy reward gets gamed (§9)Can't write down the reward → can't do RL safely
Tolerance for exploration costLearning means taking knowingly sub-optimal actions for a whileEvery action is high-stakes/irreversible → exploration is unacceptable

Where the bar is genuinely cleared (use cases):

  • Sequential recommendation — optimising a session or long-term engagement, not the next click in isolation (when the sequence genuinely matters; otherwise a contextual bandit).
  • Dynamic pricing over a horizon — where today's price affects future demand and inventory (bandit-first; RL only if the sequential coupling is real and you have the data — see Mission Patterns).
  • Inventory / supply-chain optimisation — multi-period ordering decisions under uncertainty, where a simulator of demand exists.
  • Real-time bidding (ad auctions) — sequential budget-pacing decisions at massive volume, with abundant interaction data.
  • Robotics & control — physical systems with a simulator (the textbook fit, and rarely a consulting deliverable).
  • Game AI — the famous wins (Atari, Go, StarCraft); a perfect simulator and unlimited cheap experience.
  • RLHF for LLMs — aligning model outputs to human preferences via PPO; the most commercially relevant RL most leaders will touch, and it is done by frontier labs, not assembled per-client (see Large Language Models in Practice).

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: Notice what every genuine fit shares: cheap, abundant experience (a simulator or firehose of data) and a reward you can trust. Strip either away and full RL collapses. Almost every business problem fails one of those two tests — which is why your default is to de-escalate.

subsections.title: Why RL Is Usually Overkill — and the Risks

blocks.html: Even when a problem looks sequential, full RL is usually the wrong answer, and a competent leader should be able to say why in one breath. The risks are not edge cases; they are the normal experience of trying to run RL on a real business problem.

RiskWhat goes wrongConsequence for a mission
Needs a simulator or huge dataRL can't learn from a small static dataset; live trial-and-error on real operations is slow and costlyWithout one of these, the project is infeasible — stop here
Reward hacking / specification gamingThe agent maximises the reward you wrote, not the outcome you meant — finding loopholes you never imaginedOptimises a proxy to the detriment of the real goal; can be catastrophic and is hard to foresee (see Responsible AI: Interpretability, Fairness, Safety & the EU AI Act)
Safety & exploration costExploration means taking bad actions on purpose; on live systems that's real lost money or harmUnacceptable for high-stakes, irreversible decisions without a simulator
Hard to evaluate & debugNon-deterministic, no fixed test set, failures can come from reward, environment, or policy"It worked in the sim" rarely transfers; offline evaluation of an RL policy is a research problem in itself
Non-stationarityThe environment shifts (markets, users, competitors react), so a policy learned yesterday degradesContinuous retraining and monitoring; the agent can also change the environment it's learning from
Talent & costDeep RL expertise is scarce and expensive; training runs are long and finickyHigh build cost, long timelines, key-person risk

blocks.title: reward hacking, concretely
blocks.html: A cleaning robot rewarded for "no visible mess" learns to cover the mess or disable its own camera; a recommender rewarded for clicks learns to serve outrage-bait; a trading agent rewarded for short-term P&L learns to take hidden tail risk. The agent isn't malfunctioning — it is doing exactly what you rewarded, which is rarely exactly what you wanted. This is specification gaming, and it is the single most important RL risk for a leader to internalise, because it doesn't show up in the demo.

The default position, stated plainly: most problems pitched as "RL" are really one of three lighter things —

  • Supervised learning — if you actually have (or can collect) labelled examples of the right decision, predict it directly. Far cheaper, testable, debuggable. (Most "the agent should decide X" problems are this.)
  • A bandit — if it's a one-shot choice scored immediately (recommendation, content, offer, price test), use §7. This covers the large majority of "optimise the decision" requests.
  • Optimisation / rules / a simple heuristic — if the dynamics are known, an operations-research solver or a good rule often beats a learned policy and is auditable.

Full RL earns its place only when the problem is irreducibly sequential, you can generate cheap experience, you can define a trustworthy reward, and the value justifies the cost and risk. That is a short list. Your most valuable contribution is usually talking a client down from RL into a bandit or a supervised model — the same de-escalation reflex as the agents module (Agentic Architectures & LLM App Patterns) and the value/complexity budget the whole path runs on.

Sell optimised decisions, not "reinforcement learning." Lead with the bandit; treat full RL as a conclusion you reach only after a feasibility gate, never the opening pitch.

  • Contextual-Bandit Personalisation / Recommendation — Problem: the client wants to personalise a recommendation, homepage, feed, or notification and is currently using fixed rules or a stale A/B test. Deliverable: a contextual-bandit personalisation engine — features defined, arms (content/items) wired, Thompson-sampling or UCB policy, an online evaluation harness reporting regret and lift vs. the current baseline, integrated into the serving path. Scope: 6–10 weeks for a single well-bounded surface. Who buys: the product, growth, or e-commerce lead who owns the conversion/engagement metric.
  • Dynamic Pricing or Offer Optimisation (bandit-first, RL only if justified) — Problem: "optimise our pricing / which discount / which offer." Deliverable: a bandit-based offer/price optimiser that adaptively allocates across a discrete set of price points or offers, optimising margin or conversion, with guardrails (price floors/ceilings, fairness and legal review). The proposal explicitly scopes full sequential RL as a later phase, gated on proven need and data. Scope: 8–12 weeks for the bandit; RL phase quoted separately and only after the gate. Who buys: the commercial/revenue/pricing owner.
  • RL Feasibility Assessment (usually de-escalates to bandits/supervised) — Problem: a client (or a vendor, or an excited exec) wants "an RL system." Deliverable: a scored feasibility assessment against the four-condition bar (§8) — is it truly sequential? is there a simulator or enough data? is the reward definable and safe? can they tolerate exploration cost? — with a recommended path that, in most cases, is a bandit or a supervised model, plus a phased plan and risk register (reward-hacking, safety, evaluation). Scope: 2–4 weeks, fixed price. Who buys: a CTO/CDO who needs a credible reality check before funding an RL programme.

Buy-vs-build for the client: the algorithms (UCB, Thompson sampling, PPO) and the RL/bandit libraries are commodities — rent them. What you build and charge for is the problem framing (bandit vs RL vs supervised), the reward/metric definition, the context features, the guardrails, and the online evaluation harness — the work that makes it correct and safe on their problem. What to charge for: the feasibility assessment and the bandit engine as named, fixed-scope deliverables; the online-evaluation harness (regret/lift) is concrete, repeatable IP. What NOT to promise: autonomous RL in production quickly — it is rarely quick, rarely cheap, and rarely the right answer; a specific reward number before you've seen real interaction data; or that exploration is free. Commit to a bandit-first method, a measured uplift, and an RL gate — never to a deployed autonomous RL agent on a timeline.
As of 2026 — treat names/capabilities as perishable; re-check before quoting.

Tool / CategoryUse hereOSS / PaidNote
Bandit libraries (Vowpal Wabbit, contextual-bandit modules in MLlib, bespoke)Contextual bandits for recommendation/offersOSSThe practical workhorse; usually all you need — start here
Personalisation / decisioning platforms (Azure Personalizer-style services, vendor decisioning suites)Managed contextual bandits behind an APIPaid (consumption)Fast to ship; watch lock-in, data residency, and limited control of the policy
Experimentation platforms (Optimizely, Statsig, GrowthBook, etc.)Many now offer bandit/adaptive allocation alongside A/BPaid / OSS mixOften the fastest route to a bandit if one's already in the stack — check before building
RL libraries (Stable-Baselines3, RLlib/Ray, CleanRL)Full RL (DQN, PPO, actor-critic) when the bar is clearedOSSPowerful; the easy part. The hard part is the simulator, reward, and evaluation
Simulators / environments (Gymnasium, PettingZoo, custom domain sims)The environment RL trains againstOSS / bespokeThe real bottleneck — no good simulator usually means no RL
Cloud RL / training (managed training on AWS/Azure/GCP)Compute for long RL training runsPaid (consumption)Long, finicky runs; cost and key-person risk dominate

The pattern holds: buy the commodity (libraries, decisioning services), build the differentiator (your problem framing, reward, features, guardrails, and evaluation). The perishable names live here; the judgment — bandit first, RL only past the gate — does not.
Full RL is overkill far more often than not. Read top to bottom and stop at the first row that fits.

SituationRL is overkill — do this instead
Each decision is one-shot, scored immediately (recommend, content, offer, price test)Use a contextual bandit (§7) — RL lite, far cheaper and safer
You have (or can label) examples of the right decisionSupervised learning — predict the decision directly; testable and debuggable
You just need to prove one change caused a liftA rigorous A/B test — see Model Evaluation, Validation & Experimentation
The dynamics are fully known and writableOptimisation / operations research or a rule — auditable, no exploration cost
No simulator and no large interaction datasetStop — RL is infeasible; reframe as supervised, bandit, or a data-collection mission
The reward can't be defined without obvious loopholesDon't do RL — a hackable reward will be hacked (Responsible AI: Interpretability, Fairness, Safety & the EU AI Act)
Decisions are high-stakes and irreversible, with no simKeep a human deciding; exploration cost is unacceptable on live operations

The most senior thing you can say about an RL request is "this is a bandit" — or "this is supervised learning." It is almost always cheaper, safer, more measurable, and easier to sell with a straight face.

• Sutton & Barto — Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction — the canonical text; read the first chapters for the setup, value functions, and bandits (it opens with bandits for a reason). (Free online)
  • Lattimore & Szepesvári — Bandit Algorithms — the reference for the part you'll actually use. (Free online)

• David Silver's RL Course (DeepMind/UCL) — the standard lecture series for the foundations.
  • Spinning Up in Deep RL (OpenAI) — a pragmatic on-ramp to deep RL (DQN, PPO, actor-critic) if a mission ever clears the bar.
  • Industry write-ups on contextual bandits for recommendation/personalisation and on specification gaming / reward hacking — for the practical and the cautionary sides. Date everything.

• Deeper math (MDPs, Bellman optimality, convergence) → Reinforcement Learning Foundations (AI Scientist Path).
  • Deep RL theory (DQN, policy gradients, PPO, actor-critic, exploration) → Deep Reinforcement Learning (AI Scientist Path).
  • Deeper code & advanced applied topics → Advanced Topics (ML Engineer Path).
  • Adjacent on this path: experimentation & A/B vs bandits → Model Evaluation, Validation & Experimentation; RLHF in context → Large Language Models in Practice; reward hacking & safety → Responsible AI: Interpretability, Fairness, Safety & the EU AI Act.

items.html: Is each decision one-shot and scored soon after? → Contextual bandit, not RL.
Do you have (or can you label) examples of the right decision? → Supervised learning, not RL.
Do you only need to prove one change caused a lift? → A/B test, not a bandit.
Is the problem genuinely sequential — does today's action change tomorrow's state?
Is there a simulator or a firehose of interaction data to learn from? (No → stop.)
Can you write down a reward you'd trust an adversary to optimise? (No → stop.)
Can you tolerate exploration cost (knowingly sub-optimal live actions)?
Have you priced online evaluation (regret/lift) and monitoring for non-stationarity?

blocks.html:
• "Let's build an RL system" — before anyone has tried a bandit or a supervised model.
  • "We'll just define a reward" — said lightly, with no thought to how it gets gamed.
  • No simulator and only a small static dataset — RL cannot learn from that.
  • Success defined as "use RL," not as a measured uplift over the current baseline.
  • A fixed 50/50 A/B test running for weeks where a bandit would have earned while learning.
  • Exploration cost ignored — no one has priced the sub-optimal actions learning requires.
  • "It worked in the simulator" treated as proof it will work live (it usually isn't).
  • A high-stakes, irreversible decision proposed for autonomous learning with no human gate.

• "Is this one decision scored immediately, or a sequence where each choice changes what comes next?"
  • "Do you have examples of the right answer, or only outcomes you can score after the fact?"
  • "What exactly is the reward — and how could a clever system maximise it while defeating your real goal?"
  • "Where will the millions of trials come from — a simulator, or live traffic, and at what cost per mistake?"
  • "What does 'better' mean in a number, and how will we measure uplift against today's baseline?"
  • "What's the worst action this could take, and is it reversible?"
  • "Would a contextual bandit on this surface get you most of the value, far sooner and far cheaper?"
Before closing out the path, you should be able to:

items.html: Explain the RL loop (state → action → reward → update) and read the return, Bellman, and Q-learning formulas without deriving them
Tell a one-shot bandit problem from a genuinely sequential RL problem, and both from a supervised one
Argue, to a stakeholder, why a contextual bandit beats a fixed A/B test for personalisation — and when an A/B test is still the right call
State the four-condition bar full RL must clear, and recognise that most problems fail it
Explain reward hacking / specification gaming with a concrete example and connect it to safety and the EU AI Act
Scope a bandit-first personalisation or pricing mission and say plainly what you will not promise — especially "autonomous RL in production quickly"
